passages.title: From Letters to Video Calls
passages.text:
(1) Long ago, people had fewer ways to communicate with friends and family who lived far away. If someone wanted to share news, they often wrote a letter. The letter traveled by horse, train, or mail truck before reaching its destination. Sometimes people waited days or even weeks to receive a reply.

(2) As technology improved, new inventions changed how people communicated. Telephones allowed people to hear each other's voices without being in the same place. Later, computers and the internet made it possible to send messages in just a few seconds. Families could share news much more quickly than before.

(3) Today, many people use smartphones, tablets, and computers to stay connected. Video calls allow friends and family to see and talk to each other even when they are hundreds of miles apart. Students can learn online, and people can share photos and videos with just a few taps on a screen.

(4) These changes have made daily life different in many ways. People can communicate faster, find information more easily, and connect with others around the world. While letters are still used today, technology has given people many new ways to stay in touch.

passages.title: Jobs Around the World
passages.text:
(1) The place where people live can affect the work they do. Different places have different land, weather, and natural resources. These human and physical characteristics often help determine which jobs are common in a community.

(2) In coastal towns near the ocean, many people work in jobs connected to the water. Some people catch fish, while others operate boats or work at harbors. Tourism is also important in many coastal areas. Visitors often travel to beaches, creating jobs for hotel workers, restaurant employees, and tour guides.

(3) In farming regions, the land and climate help crops grow. Farmers plant and harvest fruits, vegetables, and grains. Other workers drive farm equipment, transport crops, or help sell food at markets. The work people do depends on the type of crops that can grow in that area.

(4) Large cities often have different types of jobs. Because many people live and work close together, cities may have offices, stores, hospitals, schools, and factories. People might work as teachers, nurses, engineers, construction workers, or business owners. The large population creates a need for many different services.

(5) Whether people live near the ocean, on farmland, or in a busy city, the characteristics of a place can influence the jobs available there. The environment and the needs of the community often work together to shape how people earn a living.

passages.title: Planting Trees in the Neighborhood
passages.text:
(1) Several years ago, a neighborhood park had very few trees. During the summer, the playground became hot because there was little shade. Birds and squirrels were not often seen in the area, and many people chose to visit other parks instead.

(2) Community members decided they wanted to improve the park. Families, teachers, and students worked together to plant young trees around the playground and walking paths. Volunteers watered the trees and helped care for them as they grew.

(3) Over time, the park began to change. The growing trees provided shade on warm days. More birds and small animals returned to the area. Families visited more often, and children had a cooler place to play.

(4) By planting trees, the community changed the physical environment in a positive way. Their efforts improved the park and made it more enjoyable for both people and wildlife.

passages.title: Trash Along the River
passages.text:
(5) A river flowed through a small town and provided water for plants and animals. People enjoyed fishing near the river and walking along its banks. For many years, the area remained clean and healthy.

(6) As the town grew, more people began visiting the river. Unfortunately, some visitors left behind plastic bottles, food wrappers, and other trash. During rainstorms, litter washed into the water.

(7) The pollution affected the environment. Some animals had difficulty finding clean places to live, and trash collected along the shoreline. People also noticed that the river looked less beautiful than it once had.

(8) Community leaders encouraged residents to help clean the area. Volunteers collected trash and placed recycling bins near popular gathering spots. Although the cleanup helped, many people realized that preventing pollution was just as important as removing it.

(9) The river showed how human activities can change the physical environment in negative ways. It also showed that people can work together to improve environmental problems.

passages.title: A New Pond for Wildlife
passages.text:
(1) A school sat near an empty field that was covered with short grass. Although students enjoyed playing outside, there were not many animals in the area. Teachers noticed that birds rarely stopped there, and students seldom saw frogs, turtles, or insects.

(2) One year, the community decided to create a small pond beside the field. Workers carefully dug the pond and planted native plants around the edges. Students helped by spreading seeds and learning how to care for the new habitat.

(3) Over the next few months, the area began to change. Rain filled the pond, and plants started to grow. Soon, dragonflies buzzed above the water. Frogs appeared along the shore, and ducks occasionally stopped to rest.

(4) Students enjoyed observing the wildlife during science lessons. The pond provided water for animals and created a new habitat where many living things could survive. By changing the land in a thoughtful way, people helped improve the local environment.

passages.title: Too Many Trails
passages.text:
(5) A forest near a town became a popular place for hiking and biking. At first, visitors stayed on the marked trails. The forest remained healthy, and many plants and animals lived there.

(6) As more people visited the area, some began creating their own paths through the woods. New trails appeared in places where plants once grew. People sometimes walked over young trees and flowers without realizing the damage they were causing.

(7) After several years, parts of the forest looked different. Some plants had disappeared, and areas of bare soil appeared where vegetation once grew. During heavy rains, soil washed away more easily because fewer plants remained to hold it in place.

(8) Park workers posted signs asking visitors to stay on the marked trails. Volunteers also planted new vegetation in damaged areas. Slowly, parts of the forest began to recover.

(9) The forest showed that human activities can change the environment in negative ways, even when people do not mean to cause harm. It also showed that people can take steps to repair environmental damage.

passages.title: The Air Around Us
passages.text:
(1) The atmosphere is the layer of air that surrounds Earth. Although people cannot usually see air, it is all around us. The atmosphere stretches from the ground high into the sky. It helps make life on Earth possible.

(2) Air is made up of different gases. Most of the atmosphere is made of a gas called nitrogen. Another important gas is oxygen. People and animals need oxygen to breathe. Plants also depend on the gases in the atmosphere to grow and survive.

(3) The atmosphere does more than provide air to breathe. It helps keep Earth warm enough for living things. It also protects the planet from some harmful energy that comes from the Sun. Without the atmosphere, Earth would be a very different place.

(4) Weather happens in the atmosphere, too. Clouds, wind, rain, and snow all form in the air around us. Meteorologists, or weather scientists, study the atmosphere to learn about weather patterns and make forecasts.

(5) Every time people take a breath, fly a kite, or watch clouds move across the sky, they are experiencing the atmosphere. Even though it is invisible, the atmosphere is an important part of daily life on Earth.

passages.title: Water in the Air
passages.text:
(1) When people think about water, they often think about oceans, lakes, rivers, or puddles. However, water can also be found in the atmosphere. The atmosphere is the layer of air that surrounds Earth. Even when the sky looks clear, small amounts of water are often present in the air around us.

(2) Water in the atmosphere is called water vapor. Water vapor is a gas, so people usually cannot see it. It forms when liquid water from oceans, lakes, rivers, and other sources warms up and changes into a gas. This process is called evaporation.

(3) As water vapor rises into the atmosphere, it cools. Tiny drops of water may form and gather together. When many tiny droplets collect in one place, clouds can form. Different types of clouds may look fluffy, thin, or gray, depending on the weather.

(4) Water in the atmosphere is an important part of the water cycle. After clouds form, some of the water may fall back to Earth as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. This process is called precipitation. The water can then collect in lakes, rivers, and oceans before the cycle begins again.

(5) The next time you see clouds floating across the sky, remember that they are made of water that was once on Earth's surface. Even though water vapor is often invisible, water is present in the atmosphere and plays an important role in Earth's weather and water cycle.

passages.title: Warm Days and Cool Days
passages.text:
(1) The Sun gives Earth energy. On days when the Sun's energy warms the ground and air, temperatures can rise. During the summer, many places receive more energy from the Sun. As a result, the weather is often warmer. People may wear shorts, play outside, or look for shade on hot afternoons.

(2) At other times of the year, the Sun's energy reaches Earth at a different angle. The ground and air do not warm as much, so temperatures stay cooler. People may wear jackets and spend less time outdoors. These changes happen over weeks and months and help create different seasons.

(3) Changes in the amount of energy Earth receives can lead to longer-term weather patterns. Warm periods and cool periods are examples of weather changes that can last for many days or even months.

passages.title: Clouds and Storms
passages.text:
(4) Weather can also change over a short period of time. On some days, the sky is clear in the morning but cloudy by the afternoon. Energy from the Sun warms the air near Earth's surface. Warm air rises, and cooler air moves into its place.

(5) As warm air rises, clouds can begin to form. If enough water gathers in the clouds, rain may fall. Sometimes strong winds, thunder, and lightning can develop as part of a storm.

(6) After a storm passes, the weather may feel very different. The air can become cooler, and the sky may clear again. These short-term weather changes can happen within just a few hours.

(7) Both long-term and short-term weather changes are connected to energy. Energy from the Sun helps warm Earth's surface, while changes in the atmosphere can lead to clouds, rain, wind, and storms.

passages.title: Ancient Giants
passages.text:
(1) Millions of years ago, dinosaurs lived on Earth. Some dinosaurs were as tall as buildings, while others were no larger than a chicken. They lived in many different environments and ate different kinds of food. For a long time, dinosaurs were successful animals.

(2) Then a major event changed life on Earth. Scientists believe a large asteroid struck Earth about 66 million years ago. The impact caused big changes to the environment. Many plants and animals could not survive these changes.

(3) Dinosaurs became extinct, which means they no longer live on Earth today. Although people can see dinosaur fossils, they cannot see living dinosaurs in nature. Fossils help scientists learn about animals that lived long ago.

(4) Not all ancient organisms disappeared. Some groups of living things survived and changed over time. Scientists study fossils to understand how life on Earth has changed through millions of years.

passages.title: Living Relatives
passages.text:
(5) Many organisms alive today have ancestors that lived long ago. For example, birds share some features with certain dinosaurs. Scientists have found fossils that show similarities between ancient animals and modern birds.

(6) Over many generations, organisms can change. Some traits help organisms survive in their environments. These traits may be passed from parents to offspring. As a result, groups of organisms can look different from their ancient ancestors.

(7) Today, people can see many kinds of birds living around the world. Although birds are not dinosaurs, scientists believe some birds and certain dinosaurs are related through long lines of ancestors.

(8) Scientists also know that not every organism from the past has living relatives today. Some organisms became extinct and left no living descendants. Fossils provide clues about these ancient forms of life and help scientists understand Earth's history.

(9) By studying both fossils and living organisms, scientists learn how life has changed over time. They can see that some organisms alive today are connected to ancestors from the past, while other ancient organisms are no longer represented by living things.

passages.title: The Little Seed
passages.text:
(1) A little seed slept in the ground,(2) Quiet and safe without a sound.

(3) Rain fell softly from the sky.(4) Clouds drifted slowly way up high.

(5) Warm sunshine reached the sleeping seed.(6) It gave the plant the help it needed.

(7) Soon a tiny sprout appeared.(8) It stretched up high and showed no fear.

(9) Day by day, the plant grew tall.(10) It stood through sunshine, wind, and all.

(11) Green leaves opened wide and bright.(12) They soaked up sunshine every light.

(13) At last a flower bloomed one day.(14) Its colorful petals swayed and played.

(15) Bees buzzed near and butterflies flew.(16) The little seed had grown and grew.

(17) What began so small and deep(18) Became a flower tall and sweet.

(19) The seed had changed through work and time.(20) Its growing journey was a climb.

passages.text:

(3) Scooters zip and riders glide.(4) Smiles grow wide from side to side.

(5) Click-clack, click-clack, wheels spin fast.(6) Some rides are quick, and some rides last.

(7) Tyler tries a brand-new trick.(8) He misses once, but learns it quick.

(9) Up he rolls and down he flies.(10) His friends all cheer and shout, "Nice try!"

(11) Click-clack, click-clack, hear that sound.(12) It echoes softly all around.

(13) Brave boards bounce and racers race.(14) Busy wheels sweep through the place.

(15) As the afternoon slips away,(16) Riders enjoy one more play.

(17) The ramps grow quiet. The park grows still.(18) Tomorrow the riders will return the hill.

passages.title: The Dragon's Missing Flame
passages.text:
(1) In the magical land of Skyvale, dragons soared through the clouds every day. Some dragons breathed blue fire, while others breathed green or gold flames. The colorful flames lit up the sky each evening and made the kingdom sparkle.

(2) One morning, a young dragon named Ember woke up and tried to breathe fire. She puffed out her cheeks and took a deep breath.

(3) "Pffff!"

(4) Only a tiny cloud of smoke drifted from her nose.

(5) Ember tried again. "Pffff!"

(6) Still no flame appeared.

(7) Worried, Ember flew to the Crystal Cave, where an old wizard named Rowan lived. The wizard listened carefully as Ember explained the problem.

(8) "Have you visited the Flame Flower Field lately?" Rowan asked.

(9) Ember shook her head. "No. Why?"

(10) "Dragon flames grow strong when dragons spend time near Flame Flowers," the wizard explained. "The flowers fill the air with magical sparks."

(11) Ember thanked him and flew toward the field. Along the way, she crossed a rainbow river and soared above floating islands. Soon she reached a valley covered with bright orange Flame Flowers.

(12) Tiny golden sparks danced above the petals. Ember spent the afternoon exploring the field and playing among the glowing blossoms.

(13) As the sun began to set, she felt a warm tingling in her throat.

(14) "Maybe it's working," she whispered.

(15) Ember took a deep breath and opened her mouth.

(16) WHOOSH!

(17) A beautiful stream of golden fire burst into the air. It sparkled like a thousand tiny stars.

(18) Ember laughed with joy. She thanked the Flame Flowers and flew home through the evening sky.

(19) That night, the dragons gathered above Skyvale. When it was Ember's turn, she breathed her golden flame high into the air. The other dragons cheered as the sky shimmered with light.

(20) Ember learned that sometimes solving a problem takes patience, help from others, and a little bit of magic.

passages.title: The One-Room Schoolhouse
passages.text:
(1) In the spring of 1895, eight-year-old Clara walked along a dirt road toward her schoolhouse. The small wooden building stood at the edge of a farming community. Unlike many schools today, it had only one room. Students of different ages learned together with a single teacher.

(2) Clara carried a lunch pail and a small stack of books. As she entered the schoolhouse, she greeted her classmates and hung her coat by the door. A potbellied stove stood near the center of the room. During cold weather, it helped keep everyone warm.

(3) When the school day began, the teacher rang a handbell. The younger students practiced reading while older students worked on arithmetic problems. Because there were students of many ages, the teacher often met with small groups throughout the day.

(4) Clara enjoyed reading lessons the most. She listened carefully as students took turns reading aloud from their books. Later, she practiced writing neatly on a slate board. If she made a mistake, she could erase it and try again.

(5) At lunchtime, the students hurried outside. Some played games in the field beside the schoolhouse. Others sat beneath a tree and shared stories about life on nearby farms. The laughter and conversation filled the warm spring air.

(6) In the afternoon, the class learned about geography. The teacher pointed to places on a large wall map and explained how people traveled between towns and cities. Clara imagined what it would be like to visit distant places she had only read about.

(7) As the day ended, the teacher dismissed the students. Clara gathered her books and started the walk home. The setting sun cast long shadows across the fields. Along the way, she thought about the stories she had read and the lessons she had learned.

(8) Although Clara's school looked very different from many schools today, students still came to learn, make friends, and prepare for the future. The one-room schoolhouse was an important part of life in many communities during the late 1800s.

passages.title: The Missing Library Book
passages.text:
(1) Ava loved visiting the library after school. Every week, she borrowed new books and carefully returned them on time. One afternoon, she checked out a mystery novel she had been waiting to read for weeks.

(2) That evening, Ava read several chapters before placing the book on a shelf in her room. The next day, she planned to continue reading. However, when she looked for the book after school, it was gone.

(3) Ava searched her room, the living room, and even the kitchen. She checked under furniture and inside backpacks. The book seemed to have disappeared.

(4) As the return date grew closer, Ava became worried. She knew library books belonged to everyone in the community. If she could not find it, other readers would not be able to enjoy the story.

(5) Finally, Ava remembered that her younger brother had been playing near her bookshelf. Together, they searched his room. The missing book was tucked beneath a pile of board games.

(6) Relieved, Ava returned the book to the library the next day. She was glad she had not given up looking for it. The experience reminded her that being responsible sometimes means putting in extra effort to solve a problem.

passages.title: The Soccer Practice
passages.text:
(7) Jordan looked forward to soccer practice all week. He enjoyed playing with his teammates and learning new skills. One afternoon, he packed his cleats, water bottle, and soccer ball before heading to school.

(8) After classes ended, Jordan hurried to the field. As practice was about to begin, he opened his backpack and realized something was missing. His soccer ball was not inside.

(9) Jordan felt frustrated. He remembered using the ball in his backyard the night before. He must have left it outside.

(10) For a moment, he considered sitting out during practice. Then he decided to ask a teammate if they could share a ball.

(11) His teammate agreed right away. The two players took turns during drills and helped each other practice new moves. By the end of practice, both players had improved their skills.

(12) When Jordan arrived home, he found the ball exactly where he had left it. He promised himself that he would double-check his equipment before future practices.

(13) Although forgetting the ball created a problem, Jordan's decision to ask for help allowed him to participate and enjoy practice.

questions.text:
What did Ava do when she could not find her library book?
A  She bought a new book.
B  She looked all around her house. ✓
C  She told the library to wait.
D  She gave up looking for it.

his favorite

A  Being responsible can take extra work. (6) ✓
B  Asking for help can solve a problem. (13) ✓
C  It is fun to play soccer with friends. (7)
D  Ava read several chapters at night. (2)
E  Reading books in your room is quiet. (2)

passages.title: The Moon Garden
passages.text:
(1) In the year 2150, people lived in cities on the Moon. Large glass domes protected the cities from the harsh environment outside. Inside the domes, families lived, worked, and attended school.

(2) Ten-year-old Leo loved helping in the Moon Garden. The garden was filled with vegetables, flowers, and fruit trees. Special lights provided the energy plants needed to grow.

(3) One morning, Leo noticed that several plants looked weak. Their leaves were drooping, and some flowers had stopped blooming. He checked the control screen and discovered that one section of the garden was not receiving enough light.

(4) Leo reported the problem to the garden manager. Together, they examined the equipment and found a damaged light panel. The manager replaced the panel, and the plants soon began to recover.

(5) A week later, the garden was healthy again. Bright green leaves filled the growing area, and colorful flowers bloomed beneath the lights. Leo felt proud that he had noticed the problem before it became worse.

(6) Living on the Moon required people to work together and use technology carefully. Even small problems could affect daily life inside the dome.

passages.title: The Ocean City
passages.text:
(7) Far beneath the ocean's surface, the city of Seawatch rested inside a series of strong glass tunnels and buildings. Scientists, engineers, and families lived there while studying ocean life.

(8) Maya enjoyed exploring the city's observation halls. Through the glass walls, she could watch fish, sea turtles, and other animals swim through the water.

(9) One afternoon, Maya noticed that a sensor near an observation window was flashing. The sensor helped monitor the water pressure outside the city.

(10) She quickly informed an engineer. After checking the equipment, the engineer discovered that the sensor was giving incorrect readings. Although the city was safe, the faulty sensor needed to be repaired.

(11) The engineer thanked Maya for speaking up. If the problem had gone unnoticed, workers might not have received accurate information about conditions outside the city.

(12) That evening, Maya watched schools of fish glide past the windows. She smiled, knowing she had helped keep the city running smoothly.

(13) In Seawatch, advanced technology made life beneath the ocean possible. People depended on both technology and teamwork to stay safe and successful.

title: Anansi and the Basket of Wisdom

passages.title: Anansi and the Basket of Wisdom
passages.text:
(1) Long ago in West Africa, there lived a clever spider named Anansi. Anansi loved collecting things, especially things that might make him seem important. One day, he heard that a magical basket held all the wisdom in the world.

(2) Anansi searched high and low until he found the basket. When he looked inside, he smiled. "Now I will be the wisest creature in the world!" he said.

(3) Anansi did not want anyone else to have the wisdom. He decided to hide the basket at the top of a tall tree. He tied the basket to the front of his body and began to climb.

(4) Up he went. The basket bumped against the tree trunk. It got in his way every time he tried to climb higher.

(5) Anansi grumbled and tried again. He pulled himself up a few more feet, but the basket kept getting in the way.

(6) Nearby, Anansi's young son watched quietly.

(7) "Father," he called, "why don't you tie the basket to your back instead of your front?"

(8) Anansi stopped climbing. He thought about his son's idea. Then he moved the basket to his back and quickly climbed much higher.

(9) Suddenly, Anansi became upset. He had spent so much time trying to collect all the wisdom in the world, yet his son had thought of a better solution than he had.

(10) "If my son can think of something I did not," Anansi said, "then one creature cannot hold all the wisdom."

(11) In his frustration, Anansi tossed the basket into the air. The basket opened, and wisdom scattered across the land.

(12) Some wisdom landed in villages. Some landed in forests. Some landed near rivers and mountains. Soon, people everywhere had a chance to learn and share ideas.

(13) From that day on, wisdom belonged to everyone instead of just one creature. People passed knowledge from one generation to the next, and communities grew stronger by learning from one another.

(14) Many versions of this folktale have been told for generations. The story reminds listeners that no one knows everything and that people can learn valuable lessons from one another.

questions.text:

B  So the basket will look nicer
C  So his son can carry it
D  So he can hide it in the grass

A  No one person knows everything ✓
B  Spiders are the wisest of all
C  It is best to climb trees alone
D  Baskets are hard to carry

Read the sentence. In paragraph (2), Anansi "searched high and low" for the basket. This phrase means he looked [ ▾ ].
everywhere for it ✓
one time for it
up at the sky
for a new tree

title: The Fox and the Full Cart

passages.title: The Fox and the Full Cart
passages.text:
(1) A fox was walking along a country road when he spotted a farmer's cart filled with apples. The cart was parked beside a field while the farmer worked nearby.

(2) The fox loved apples. He wanted as many as he could get.

(3) "If I take just one apple," thought the fox, "I will still be hungry. I should take a whole pile!"

(4) The fox climbed into the cart and began pushing apples toward the edge. One apple fell to the ground. Then another. Then another.

(5) Soon, apples were rolling in every direction.

(6) A rabbit hopping by stopped and watched.

(7) "Why are you throwing away so many apples?" asked the rabbit.

(8) "I am making a pile for myself," said the fox. "Then I will have more apples than anyone else."

(9) The rabbit shook his head and continued on his way.

(10) Just then, the farmer heard the apples falling. He turned around and saw the fox standing in the cart.

(11) "Hey!" shouted the farmer.

(12) The startled fox jumped from the cart and ran as fast as he could. He raced across the field and disappeared into the woods.

(13) After a while, the fox stopped to rest. He looked around and realized he had not brought a single apple with him.

(14) All the apples he had knocked from the cart were still back on the road.

(15) Meanwhile, the farmer gathered the fallen apples and placed them back in the cart.

(16) That evening, the fox sat beneath a tree with an empty stomach.

(17) "If I had taken only one apple," he said, "I would have something to eat right now."

(18) The fox learned that greed can cause people to lose what they already have.

questions.text:
What lesson does the fox learn in the story?
A  Being greedy can make you lose what you have.
B  Apples are the best food to eat. ✓
C  Rabbits are good friends to have.
D  Farmers work hard all day long.

How does the fox act when the farmer shouts at him?
A  He jumps out and runs away fast. ✓
B  He keeps pushing apples out.
C  He hides inside the cart.
D  He gives the apples back.

Read the sentence. The word startled in paragraph (12) means the fox felt [ ▾ ].
surprised and scared ✓
happy and full
sleepy and slow
calm and still

title: Johnny Kaw and the Giant Furrows

passages.title: Johnny Kaw and the Giant Furrows
passages.text:
(1) Long ago, settlers on the Great Plains told stories about a giant named Johnny Kaw. According to legend, Johnny was taller than a house and stronger than a team of oxen. He lived in what is now Kansas and spent his days helping people shape the land.

(2) One story says that Johnny used a giant plow to prepare fields for farmers. As he pulled the plow across the prairie, the ground rolled and folded beneath it. People claimed that the hills and valleys of the region were formed by the giant furrows he left behind.

(3) Another tale tells of Johnny digging streams and ponds to provide water for crops and animals. With a single scoop of his enormous shovel, he could move more dirt than a hundred workers.

(4) Of course, no giant really created the landscape of Kansas. The hills, rivers, and plains were shaped by natural forces over long periods of time. Still, people enjoyed telling stories about Johnny Kaw because he represented hard work, strength, and the determination of early settlers.

(5) Today, Johnny Kaw remains an important folk hero in Kansas. Although the stories are exaggerated, they help people remember the challenges faced by those who settled the region.

passages.title: Pecos Bill and the Wild West
passages.text:
(6) Another famous American legend tells the story of Pecos Bill, a cowboy whose adventures supposedly took place in the American Southwest. According to the stories, Bill was raised by coyotes after becoming separated from his family as a baby.

(7) As he grew older, Bill became known for his incredible skills. Legends claimed he could ride the fastest horses, lasso storms, and perform amazing feats that no ordinary person could accomplish.

(8) One story says that a huge drought threatened the land. To help the people, Pecos Bill rode across the countryside searching for water. When he found a hidden river, he supposedly dug a channel so the water could flow to farms and towns.

(9) Like Johnny Kaw, Pecos Bill was not a real historical figure. His stories grew larger with each retelling. The exaggerated adventures reflected the excitement, challenges, and imagination of life in the American West.

(10) Although the events in the stories never happened, Pecos Bill became a symbol of courage and determination. His legend continues to be shared because people enjoy tales about heroes who overcome enormous obstacles.

C  Johnny Kaw rode the fastest horses across the land.

E  Johnny Kaw made the hills and valleys with a rope.

passages.title: The Silver Swan
passages.text:
(1) Long ago, in a kingdom surrounded by lakes and forests, there lived a young girl named Eliza. She loved visiting the water near her village and watching the swans glide across its surface.

(2) One winter morning, Eliza discovered a swan trapped in thick ice near the shore. The bird struggled to move. Without hesitation, Eliza carefully chipped away the ice and helped the swan escape.

(3) The swan spread its wings and stepped onto the frozen lake. Then something amazing happened. The bird began to glow with silver light.

(4) "Thank you for your kindness," the swan said. "I am no ordinary bird. I am the guardian of this lake."

(5) With a sweep of its wings, the swan created a shining silver feather.

(6) "Keep this feather," the swan said. "If you ever face trouble, it will help you."

(7) Months later, a terrible drought struck the kingdom. Streams dried up, and crops stopped growing. Eliza remembered the feather and carried it to the lake.

(8) As she placed the feather on the water, it began to glow. Rain clouds gathered overhead, and gentle rain soon fell across the kingdom.

(9) The people celebrated, and the lake returned to normal. Eliza never saw the magical swan again, but she always remembered that kindness had led to something wonderful.

passages.title: The Enchanted Paintbrush
passages.text:
(10) In a distant village, a boy named Kai loved to draw. Every day, he filled pages with pictures of animals, trees, and mountains.

(11) One evening, an old traveler visited the village. He noticed Kai's drawings and smiled.

(12) "You have a special gift," the traveler said. Before leaving, he handed Kai a paintbrush carved from golden wood.

(13) The next morning, Kai used the brush to paint a bird. To his surprise, the bird fluttered off the page and flew into the sky.

(14) Kai soon discovered that anything he painted could become real.

(15) At first, he used the brush to help others. When a farmer needed tools, Kai painted them. When families needed food, he painted baskets of fruit.

(16) Word of the magical paintbrush spread throughout the region. People traveled from far away to ask for help.

(17) Although Kai could have used the brush to become rich, he chose to help his community instead.

(18) Over time, the village became a happier and more successful place. People remembered Kai not because of his magic but because he used it wisely.

D  She asks the swan for a feather.

B  Helping others is a good way to use a gift. (paragraphs 15, 17) ✓
C  You should keep magic a secret from everyone. (paragraph 4)

passages.title: The Lost Lunch Box
passages.text:
(1) Ben zipped up his backpack and hurried into school. It was Friday, and he was excited because his class was going on a nature walk that afternoon. Before leaving home, he had packed his favorite lunch in a bright blue lunch box.

(2) During the morning, Ben worked on math, reading, and science. When lunchtime arrived, he reached into his backpack.

(3) His lunch box was gone.

(4) Ben looked inside every pocket. He checked under his desk and inside his cubby. The lunch box was nowhere to be found.

(5) "Maybe I left it on the bus," Ben said.

(6) His friend Olivia offered to help. Together, they retraced Ben's steps. They looked in the hallway, the library, and the cafeteria.

(7) Ben started to worry. His lunch box had his name on it, but he still hoped someone would find it before the end of the day.

(8) After lunch, the class prepared for the nature walk. Just as Ben was lining up at the door, the school secretary entered the room carrying a bright blue lunch box.

(9) "Is this yours?" she asked.

(10) Ben smiled. "Yes!"

(11) The secretary explained that another student had found the lunch box near the bus area and turned it in to the office.

(12) "I'm glad someone was honest," Ben said.

(13) Later that afternoon, Ben enjoyed the nature walk with his class. As they explored the trail, he thought about the student who had returned the lunch box.

(14) When he got home, Ben told his family what had happened.

(15) "I learned two things today," he said. "I should double-check my backpack, and it's important to return things that belong to other people."

(16) His family agreed. Sometimes a small act of honesty can make a big difference.

title: A Morning at the Bus Stop

passages.title: A Morning at the Bus Stop
passages.text:
(1) The neighborhood was just beginning to wake up. Porch lights glowed softly in the early morning darkness, and the sky was painted with shades of pink, orange, and pale blue. A cool breeze rustled the leaves on nearby trees.

(2) Near the corner of the street, a small group of students waited at the bus stop. Some chatted with friends while others held backpacks close to their sides. A few parents stood nearby, waving as younger children climbed aboard the bus.

(3) Birds chirped from the branches overhead. A dog barked in the distance. The smell of fresh pancakes drifted from an open kitchen window. Cars rolled slowly down the street as people headed to work and school.

(4) The students watched for the familiar yellow bus. Every few moments, someone looked down the road and then turned back to continue a conversation. Laughter mixed with the sounds of the morning, creating a cheerful start to the day.

(5) Finally, the bus appeared around the corner. Its lights flashed as it approached the stop. Backpacks were lifted, conversations ended, and students lined up near the curb.

(6) Within a few minutes, the bus doors closed and the vehicle continued down the street. The corner became quiet again. The birds still sang, the trees still swayed, and the neighborhood slowly settled into the rhythm of an ordinary school day.

passages.title: The New Student
passages.text:
(1) On Monday morning, Emma noticed a new student sitting quietly at a desk near the window. During reading time, the student kept looking around the room but did not talk to anyone.

(2) At recess, groups of children ran to the playground. The new student stood near the fence and watched the others play.

(3) Emma remembered how nervous she had felt when she started at a new school. She walked over and introduced herself.

(4) "Would you like to play with us?" Emma asked.

(5) The student's face brightened. "Okay," he said with a smile.

(6) Soon, they were playing tag with several classmates. By the end of recess, the new student was laughing and talking with the group.

(7) After school, the student thanked Emma for including him.

(8) Emma felt happy. A simple invitation had helped someone feel welcome.

passages.title: The Forgotten Homework
passages.text:
(9) One evening, Noah finished his homework and carefully placed it inside his folder. He set the folder on the kitchen table so he would remember it the next morning.

(10) Before leaving for school, Noah grabbed his backpack and rushed out the door. He felt proud because he had completed all of his work.

(11) Later that day, the teacher asked students to turn in their homework. Noah reached into his backpack and froze.

(12) His folder was not there.

(13) Suddenly, he remembered leaving it on the kitchen table.

(14) Noah felt disappointed. He had worked hard, but he had forgotten to bring the assignment.

(15) After school, he told his family what had happened. Together, they came up with a plan. From then on, Noah packed his backpack completely before going to bed.

(16) The next morning, everything he needed was ready to go.

(17) Noah learned that being prepared can help prevent problems.

questions.text:
In Text 1, why did Emma walk over to the new student?

sleepy
angry

Each story teaches a lesson. Select two sentences that tell a lesson from the stories.

B  Being ready ahead of time can stop problems. ✓
C  Recess is the best part of school.
D  New students should sit by the window.
E  Homework should always be done at the table.

passages.title: The Ice Cream Shop
passages.text:
(1) The ice cream shop buzzed with activity on a warm summer afternoon. Families stood in line studying the menu while the smell of fresh waffle cones drifted through the air. The bright display case sparkled beneath the lights, showing rows of colorful flavors.

(2) Children pointed excitedly at swirls of chocolate, strawberry, and mint. The sound of laughter mixed with the clink of scoops against metal containers. Outside the shop, sunlight reflected off parked bicycles and cars.

(3) Near the counter, customers balanced towering cones topped with sprinkles. Some hurried outside before the ice cream could melt. Others lingered at small tables, enjoying the cool treat and the cheerful atmosphere.

passages.title: The First Snowfall
passages.text:
(4) Early one morning, tiny snowflakes drifted from a gray winter sky. The neighborhood looked quiet as the snow settled gently on rooftops, trees, and sidewalks. Everything seemed softer beneath the growing blanket of white.

(5) Bare branches held sparkling layers of snow. Footprints appeared along walkways where people had stepped outside to look around. The air felt crisp and cold, and each breath created a small cloud.

(6) The usual sounds of the neighborhood seemed distant. Cars moved slowly, and voices were quieter than usual. The fresh snow transformed familiar streets into a peaceful winter scene.

B  At a snowy park
C  At a school

passages.title: Simone Biles: Reaching New Heights
passages.text:
(1) Simone Biles is one of the most successful gymnasts in history. She was born in Ohio in 1997 and discovered gymnastics when she was a young child. After visiting a gymnastics center on a field trip, she became interested in the sport and began taking lessons.

(2) As Simone practiced, she learned new skills and became stronger. Gymnastics required balance, flexibility, and many hours of training. Some skills were difficult to master, but Simone continued working hard to improve.

(3) Over time, Simone began competing in gymnastics meets. Her powerful routines and exciting performances helped her stand out from other athletes. She won many competitions and earned medals at national and international events.

(4) Simone became known for performing skills that few gymnasts could do. In some cases, she was the first woman to successfully complete certain moves during major competitions. Her achievements inspired other athletes to challenge themselves and pursue their goals.

(5) Although Simone experienced success, she also faced challenges. Like many athletes, she had to overcome setbacks and make difficult decisions. Throughout her career, she showed determination and courage both on and off the competition floor.

(6) Today, Simone Biles is admired around the world. Many people respect her not only for her athletic accomplishments but also for her perseverance and leadership. Her story shows how dedication and hard work can help people achieve their goals.

questions.text:
Where was Simone Biles born?
A  In Ohio ✓
B  At a gym meet
C  On a field trip
D  In a gym class

B  She did not give up. ✓

E  She let others win.

easy wins
new gym skills
happy days

title: Jane Goodall: A Friend to Chimpanzees

passages.title: Jane Goodall: A Friend to Chimpanzees
passages.text:
(1) Jane Goodall is a scientist who became famous for studying chimpanzees. She was born in England in 1934. As a child, she loved animals and spent hours reading about nature. She dreamed of traveling to Africa to learn more about wildlife.

(2) In 1960, Jane traveled to Tanzania in East Africa. There, she began observing chimpanzees in a forest called Gombe. At first, the animals were nervous around her and stayed far away.

(3) Jane was patient. Day after day, she quietly watched the chimpanzees and took notes about their behavior. Over time, the animals became more comfortable with her presence.

(4) Jane made important discoveries. She observed chimpanzees using sticks to collect insects from small holes. Before then, many people believed that only humans used tools. Her observations helped scientists learn that chimpanzees were more intelligent than they had realized.

(5) As the years passed, Jane continued studying animals and teaching others about conservation. Conservation means protecting animals and their habitats. She traveled around the world, encouraging people to care for wildlife and the environment.

(6) Today, Jane Goodall is respected by scientists, students, and animal lovers everywhere. Her work helped people better understand chimpanzees and inspired many others to study and protect nature. Her story shows how curiosity, patience, and determination can lead to important discoveries.

D  a zoo

passages.title: Temple Grandin
passages.text:
(1) Temple Grandin is a scientist, inventor, and author who has helped improve the way animals are cared for on farms. She was born in 1947 and grew up loving animals. As a child, she spent a great deal of time observing how animals behaved.

(2) Temple noticed details that other people sometimes overlooked. She paid close attention to how animals reacted to sights, sounds, and movement. This helped her understand why animals might become nervous or calm in different situations.

(3) As an adult, Temple studied animal science and designed equipment that helped move animals more safely and comfortably. Many farms around the world use ideas she developed.

(4) Temple also wrote books and gave speeches to teach others about animals. Her work has helped many people better understand how animals experience the world around them.

passages.title: Steve Irwin
passages.text:
(5) Steve Irwin was a wildlife expert who became famous for teaching people about animals. He was born in Australia in 1962 and grew up helping care for wildlife at a family-owned animal park.

(6) From a young age, Steve enjoyed learning about reptiles, birds, and other animals. He spent years studying wildlife and sharing what he learned with others.

(7) Steve became known for his exciting television programs, where he introduced viewers to animals from around the world. His enthusiasm encouraged many people to become interested in nature and conservation.

(8) Throughout his life, Steve worked to protect animals and their habitats. He believed that people were more likely to care for wildlife if they learned about it and understood its importance.

(9) Today, many people remember Steve Irwin for his energy, passion, and dedication to helping animals.

questions.text:
What did Temple do as a child that helped her later work?
A  She watched how animals behaved. ✓
B  She wrote many books for kids.
C  She lived on a big farm.
D  She made television programs.

quiet
afraid

passages.title: Misty Copeland
passages.text:
(1) Misty Copeland is a ballet dancer who inspired many people through her hard work and determination. She was born in Missouri in 1982 and did not begin ballet lessons until she was thirteen years old. Many dancers start much younger, so Misty had to learn quickly.

(2) As she practiced, Misty showed great talent and dedication. She spent many hours improving her skills and learning difficult dance routines. Her teachers recognized her potential and encouraged her to continue pursuing ballet.

(3) Over time, Misty performed in larger productions and earned important roles. In 2015, she became the first Black woman to be promoted to principal dancer at the American Ballet Theatre, one of the most respected ballet companies in the United States.

(4) Today, Misty continues to perform, write books, and encourage young people to follow their dreams. Her story shows how perseverance can help people achieve challenging goals.

passages.title: José Hernández
passages.text:
(5) José Hernández is an engineer and former astronaut. He was born in California in 1962. As a child, he spent part of each year helping his family harvest crops on farms. Because his family moved often for work, attending school regularly could be difficult.

(6) José enjoyed learning about science and dreamed of traveling to space. He worked hard in school and later studied engineering. Even when challenges appeared, he remained focused on his goal.

(7) José applied to become an astronaut many times before being selected by NASA. Instead of giving up after being rejected, he continued improving his skills and gaining experience.

(8) In 2009, José traveled to space aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery. His journey showed that determination and hard work can help people accomplish their dreams.

(9) Today, José Hernández shares his story with students and encourages them to work toward their goals, no matter how difficult those goals may seem.

title: The Day the Lights Came Back

passages.title: The Day the Lights Came Back
passages.text:
(1) In December 1952, a powerful storm swept across parts of New England. Heavy snow fell for hours, covering roads, trees, and rooftops. Strong winds blew through towns and cities, making travel difficult and knocking down power lines.

(2) As the storm grew stronger, many homes lost electricity. Lights flickered and went dark. Televisions stopped working, and electric heaters could no longer warm houses. Families gathered together and waited for the storm to pass.

(3) In one small town, workers from the electric company prepared to head out into the snow. Even though the weather was dangerous, they knew people were depending on them. They loaded trucks with tools and supplies and carefully drove along icy roads.

(4) Throughout the day, the workers searched for damaged power lines. Snow piled up around their boots as they repaired broken equipment. In some places, fallen tree branches had damaged poles. In other places, strong winds had caused wires to snap.

(5) The work was slow and difficult. The storm had affected many communities, and there was a lot to repair. Still, the crews continued working through the cold weather.

(6) As evening approached, something exciting began to happen. One by one, homes started lighting up again. Lamps glowed in living rooms. Kitchen lights turned on. Families cheered as electricity returned to their neighborhoods.

(7) By the end of the recovery effort, power had been restored to thousands of people. The storm had caused many problems, but it also showed how communities can work together during difficult times.

(8) Today, storms still sometimes cause power outages. When they do, utility workers continue the important job of repairing equipment and restoring electricity. Their efforts help communities return to normal after severe weather.

passages.title: Trapped in a Cave
passages.text:
(1) In 2018, a soccer team in Thailand went exploring in a large cave with their coach. After practice, the group entered the cave to explore its winding tunnels. At first, the adventure seemed exciting and fun.

(2) While the team was inside, heavy rain began to fall outside. Water quickly flowed into the cave and filled some of the passageways. When the group tried to leave, they discovered that rising water had blocked their path.

(3) The players and their coach moved deeper into the cave to find a safe, dry area. There they waited and hoped rescuers would find them. Although the situation was frightening, the coach encouraged everyone to stay calm and work together.

(4) Soon, family members realized the team was missing. Rescue workers began searching the cave. Experts from several countries traveled to Thailand to help. The cave was dark, flooded, and difficult to navigate, making the rescue very challenging.

(5) For many days, rescuers searched through the underground passages. Finally, divers located the team. The players and their coach were tired, but they were safe.

(6) Bringing everyone out of the cave was not easy. Rescue teams carefully planned each step. Divers guided the players through the flooded tunnels one at a time. It took several days to complete the rescue.

(7) At last, every member of the team and their coach reached safety. People around the world celebrated the successful rescue. The event showed how courage, teamwork, and careful planning can help people overcome difficult challenges.

(8) Today, the rescue is remembered as one of the most remarkable survival stories in recent history. The players stayed calm during a frightening situation, and rescuers worked together to bring them home safely.

passages.title: Across the Antarctic Ice
passages.text:
(1) In 1914, explorer Ernest Shackleton and his crew set out on a journey to Antarctica. Their goal was to cross the frozen continent. They traveled aboard a ship called the Endurance.

(2) As the ship moved through icy waters, thick sea ice began to surround it. Soon, the Endurance became trapped. The ice squeezed the ship until it was badly damaged.

(3) Shackleton and his crew had to leave the ship and live on the ice. The weather was bitterly cold, and food was limited. Even so, the group worked together and stayed focused on survival.

(4) For months, they camped on floating ice and traveled across dangerous waters in small boats. Shackleton encouraged his crew and helped them stay hopeful during the difficult journey.

(5) After a long rescue effort, every member of the expedition survived. Although the explorers never crossed Antarctica, their teamwork and determination helped them overcome incredible challenges.

passages.title: Lost in the Amazon
passages.text:
(6) In 1981, a young woman named Juliane Koepcke was traveling on an airplane above the Amazon rainforest in South America. During a severe storm, the plane broke apart in the air.

(7) Juliane survived the crash and found herself alone in the rainforest. She had only a few supplies and was surrounded by thick jungle.

(8) Instead of panicking, Juliane used what she had learned from her parents, who were scientists. She knew that following a stream could help lead her toward people.

(9) For days, she walked through the rainforest. She faced insects, heat, and exhaustion. Even when she felt discouraged, she continued moving forward.

(10) After more than a week alone, Juliane found a small camp used by local workers. Eventually, rescuers helped her return home safely.

(11) Her survival amazed people around the world. Juliane's knowledge, courage, and determination helped her overcome a dangerous situation.

passages.title: The Race Across America
passages.text:
(1) In 1860, sending a message across the United States could take many days. Letters often traveled by stagecoach or wagon. People wanted a faster way to share important news.

(2) To solve this problem, a company started the Pony Express. Young riders carried mail on horseback between Missouri and California. The route stretched across deserts, rivers, and mountains.

(3) Riders traveled day and night. At stations along the route, they quickly switched horses and continued their journey. Speed was important because people were counting on the mail to arrive quickly.

(4) The work was difficult and sometimes dangerous. Riders faced bad weather, rough trails, and long hours in the saddle. Even so, they continued moving forward.

(5) The Pony Express operated for only a short time, but it captured people's imaginations. The riders helped connect distant communities and showed how determination and hard work could overcome great challenges.

passages.title: The Bell That Traveled the Country
passages.text:
(6) In 1885, the Statue of Liberty was being completed in France. Before the statue could be assembled in New York Harbor, Americans wanted to raise money for the pedestal that would support it.

(7) To help gather support, the Liberty Bell traveled to cities across the United States. Crowds gathered wherever the famous bell appeared. People came to see it, learn about the project, and donate money.

(8) In town after town, newspapers reported on the visits. Families lined the streets, and children waved flags as the bell arrived.

(9) The journey helped spread excitement about the Statue of Liberty. As more people learned about the project, donations increased. Eventually, enough money was raised to complete the pedestal.

(10) In 1886, the Statue of Liberty was dedicated in New York Harbor. Today, it remains one of the most recognized symbols in the United States. The traveling bell helped bring communities together to support a shared goal.

passages.title: Hidden Treasures Beneath Our Feet
passages.text:
1. Have you ever wondered where the materials used to build roads, make glass, or create jewelry come from? Many of these materials come from Earth’s nonliving resources. Nonliving resources are materials found in nature that were never alive. Rocks, minerals, soil, water, and sand are all examples. These resources have different properties that make them useful in different ways.

2. One important nonliving resource is sand. Sand is made of tiny pieces of rock and minerals. It is rough and grainy. Builders use sand to help make concrete for sidewalks and buildings. Sand is also used to make glass. Because sand is found in many places, it is one of the most commonly used natural resources on Earth.

Another useful resource is clay. Clay is a type of soil that feels smooth and sticky when it is wet. People shape wet clay into pots, bowls, and other objects. When clay is heated in a kiln, it becomes hard and strong. This property makes clay a good material for making dishes, bricks, and tiles.

3. Many people think minerals are the most interesting nonliving resources. Minerals are natural materials that have specific properties, such as color, hardness, and shine. For example, quartz is a hard mineral that is used in watches and electronics. Gold is valued because it is shiny, does not rust, and can be shaped easily. Diamonds are one of the hardest minerals on Earth, making them useful for cutting tools as well as jewelry.

Water is another important nonliving resource. It may seem ordinary, but it has special properties. Water can flow, take the shape of its container, and dissolve many substances. People need water for drinking, farming, cooking, and cleaning. Plants and animals also depend on water to survive.

4. Each nonliving resource has its own properties that make it valuable. Whether it is the rough texture of sand, the stickiness of clay, the hardness of a mineral, or the flowing nature of water, these resources help people in many ways every day. Learning about their properties can help us understand why Earth’s nonliving resources are so important.

passages.title: Will There Always Be Enough?
passages.text:
1. Imagine turning on a light switch and nothing happens. Or imagine going to the gas station and finding out there is no fuel left for cars. These situations might sound surprising, but they remind us of an important fact: some of Earth's resources are limited.

2. Earth provides people with many natural resources. Natural resources are materials that come from nature and are used by people. Some resources, such as sunlight and wind, can be replaced naturally over time. Other resources, however, are limited because they take millions of years to form or exist in only certain places.

3. Coal, oil, and natural gas are examples of limited resources. These materials are called fossil fuels. They were formed from the remains of plants and animals that lived long ago. People use fossil fuels to heat homes, generate electricity, and power vehicles. Because fossil fuels take millions of years to form, people cannot quickly replace them once they are used.

4. Fresh water is another important resource. Although water covers much of Earth, only a small amount is fresh water that people can safely use for drinking, cooking, and farming. In some places, long periods without rain can cause water shortages. Communities often work hard to protect their water supplies so they will last longer.

5. Trees are a natural resource that can be replaced, but only if people manage them carefully. Trees provide wood for building homes, making furniture, and producing paper. If too many trees are cut down without planting new ones, forests can shrink. Many communities plant new trees to help replace the ones that are used.

6. People can help limited resources last longer by making wise choices. Turning off lights when they are not needed can save energy. Fixing leaking faucets can save water. Recycling paper, metal, and plastic can reduce the need for new materials. Even small actions can make a difference.

7. Earth's resources help people meet their everyday needs. Because some resources are limited, it is important to use them carefully. By understanding where resources come from and how they are used, people can help make sure these valuable materials are available for future generations.

passages.title: The Rabbits in the Snow
passages.text:
1. In a northern forest, a group of rabbits lived among trees and bushes. The rabbits were all the same kind of animal, but they were not exactly alike. Some rabbits had thick fur, while others had thinner fur. Fur thickness was an inherited trait that was passed from parents to their offspring.

2. One winter, the weather became colder than usual. Snow covered the ground for many months, and freezing temperatures lasted longer than expected. Rabbits with thicker fur stayed warmer during the cold weather. They used less energy to keep their bodies warm and were able to spend more time searching for food.

3. Rabbits with thinner fur often had a harder time surviving the long winter. Many struggled to stay warm when temperatures dropped. As a result, fewer of them survived until spring.

4. When spring arrived, many of the rabbits with thicker fur found mates and had offspring. Their young often inherited the trait for thicker fur. Over time, scientists noticed that thick fur became more common in the rabbit population. The inherited trait had given some rabbits an advantage in surviving and reproducing.

passages.title: The Tall Sunflowers
passages.text:
5. In a large field, hundreds of sunflowers grew close together. Although the plants belonged to the same species, they were not identical. Some sunflowers grew taller than others because of inherited traits they received from their parent plants.

6. As the sunflowers grew, they competed for sunlight. Taller sunflowers were able to reach more sunlight because their leaves were above many of the shorter plants. The extra sunlight helped them make more food through photosynthesis.

7. Many shorter sunflowers received less sunlight because nearby plants blocked some of the light. With less energy available, these plants often produced fewer seeds.

8. The taller sunflowers were more likely to produce large numbers of seeds. When those seeds grew into new plants, many inherited the trait for greater height. After several growing seasons, scientists observed that taller sunflowers became more common in the field. Their inherited trait had helped them survive and reproduce successfully.

passages.title: The School Talent Show
passages.text:
1. Students at Oak Hill Elementary were excited about the school's annual talent show. The event gave students a chance to share their talents with family and friends. To make the show successful, organizers relied on several forms of energy.

2. One important form of energy was light energy. Bright stage lights helped the audience see the performers clearly. Spotlights shined on singers, dancers, and musicians as they performed. Without light energy, it would have been difficult for the audience to see what was happening on stage.

3. Sound energy was just as important. Microphones helped performers' voices reach the back of the auditorium. Speakers carried music and announcements throughout the room. Sound energy allowed everyone to hear the performances and enjoy the show.

4. The talent show also used electrical energy. The lights, microphones, speakers, and electronic instruments all depended on electricity. Electrical energy powered many of the devices that made the event possible.

5. By the end of the evening, students realized that light energy, sound energy, and electrical energy had all played important roles. Each form of energy helped the talent show run smoothly and safely.

passages.title: A Day at the Science Museum
passages.text:
6. During a field trip to a science museum, students explored exhibits that demonstrated different forms of energy. Many of the displays showed how energy affects everyday life.

7. One exhibit focused on heat energy. Students used special cameras to see warm and cool objects. They learned that heat energy moves from warmer objects to cooler objects. The exhibit showed how heat energy is used to warm homes and cook food.

8. Another exhibit explored magnetic energy. Students experimented with magnets that attracted certain metals without touching them. They discovered that magnetic energy is used in electronic devices, trains, and household appliances.

9. The museum also displayed examples of electrical energy working with magnetic energy. Students learned that many machines use both forms of energy to perform tasks. The exhibits helped visitors understand that energy can appear in different forms and be used in many ways.

10. As the field trip ended, students discussed what they had learned. They agreed that heat energy and magnetic energy are important because they help people complete everyday tasks and use technology more effectively.

title: Growing and Changing: A Community Through Time

passages.title: Growing and Changing: A Community Through Time
passages.text:
1. Have you ever noticed new buildings, roads, or parks being built in your community? Communities do not stay the same forever. Over time, local communities change as people find new ways to live, work, and meet their needs.

2. Many years ago, the town of Maple Grove was a small farming community. Most families lived on farms and grew crops such as corn and wheat. There were only a few dirt roads, one general store, and a small school. Many people traveled by horse and wagon because cars were not common.

3. As more families moved to Maple Grove, the community began to grow. New homes were built, and the dirt roads were paved. Stores, restaurants, and a library opened to serve the increasing population. These changes made it easier for people to find the goods and services they needed.

4. Technology also helped change the community. Years ago, people relied on letters and landline telephones to communicate. Today, many people use computers, cell phones, and the internet. Businesses use technology to connect with customers, and students use it to learn and complete schoolwork.

5. Transportation has changed over time as well. More roads were added to handle increased traffic. Public buses began serving the area, making it easier for people to travel to work, school, and community events. Improved transportation helped connect Maple Grove to nearby towns and cities.

6. Some parts of the community have stayed the same. The town still has a school, parks, and places where people gather for celebrations. Community members continue to work together to solve problems and make the town a good place to live.

7. Today, Maple Grove looks very different from the small farming community it once was. New buildings, technology, and transportation have changed the town over time. These changes show how local communities grow and adapt to meet the needs of the people who live in them.

[ fewer roads were needed | stores, restaurants, and a library opened to serve the growing population ✅ | people stopped using technology | farms replaced most buildings ]

passages.title: Looking Back at River Town
passages.text:
1. Historians often study the past to learn how communities have changed over time. To do this, they use different kinds of sources. Two important types are primary sources and secondary sources. Both can help people understand what life was like long ago and how communities have changed.

2. A primary source is something created during the time being studied. Photographs, letters, diaries, maps, and newspaper articles are all examples of primary sources. These sources provide firsthand information about people, places, and events.

3. One primary source from River Town is a photograph taken in 1950. The picture shows a small downtown area with only a few stores and narrow streets. Most people are walking or riding bicycles. There are very few cars in the photograph. The image helps historians see what the community looked like many years ago.

4. Another primary source is a diary written by a resident named Maria Lopez. In one entry, she described visiting the town's only grocery store and attending community events at a small park. Her diary provides details about daily life that cannot be learned from a photograph alone.

5. Secondary sources can also help people learn about the past. A secondary source is created after an event or time period has ended. History books, articles, and documentaries are examples of secondary sources. These sources often use information from many primary sources to explain what happened.

6. A local historian recently wrote a book about River Town's growth. The book explains that new businesses opened, more families moved into the area, and larger roads were built. The historian used photographs, diaries, and newspaper articles to gather information before writing the book.

7. By comparing the old photograph, Maria's diary, and the historian's book, people can see how River Town has changed over time. The community now has many stores, larger parks, and busy streets filled with cars. The information from both primary and secondary sources helps tell the story of these changes.

8. Primary and secondary sources each provide valuable information. When people use both types of sources together, they can better understand how communities, people, and places have changed throughout history.

questions.text:

[ from someone who experienced it directly ✅ | from a history book written later | from several different sources | from a scientist's experiment ]

passages.title: A Photograph from Long Ago
passages.text:
1. In the town of Cedar Creek, many people were curious about what their community looked like long ago. One day, students visited a local museum and found a photograph taken in 1948. The photograph was a primary source because it was created during the time being studied.

2. The photograph showed a small downtown area with only a few buildings. There was one grocery store, a post office, and a gas station. Most of the streets were narrow, and only a handful of cars could be seen. Several people were walking along the sidewalks.

3. As students studied the photograph, they noticed other details. There were no traffic lights, and many of the buildings were only one story tall. The photograph helped students see exactly what the community looked like many years ago.

4. The students compared the photograph to present-day Cedar Creek. Today, the town has larger roads, more businesses, and many more cars. By examining the old photograph, students were able to see how the community had changed over time. The primary source provided firsthand evidence of the past.

passages.title: A Historian's Report
passages.text:
5. A local historian wanted to explain how Cedar Creek had grown during the last seventy years. After studying old photographs, letters, maps, and newspaper articles, the historian wrote a report about the town's history. The report was a secondary source because it was created after the events had already happened.

6. The report explained that new neighborhoods were built as more families moved to Cedar Creek. It also described how businesses opened to serve the growing population. The historian included information gathered from many different primary sources.

7. Students learned that a large highway was built near the town in the 1980s. The highway made it easier for people to travel and helped new businesses move into the area. The report also described how the community added schools, parks, and public services over time.

8. By reading the historian's report, students gained information about changes that happened across many years. The secondary source combined information from several primary sources to explain how the community developed. Together, both types of sources helped students understand how Cedar Creek changed over time.

questions.text:
How are the two passages different?
A. Passage 1 explains changes that happened after the 1980s, while Passage 2 focuses on events before 1948.B. Passage 1 uses a primary source to show what Cedar Creek looked like long ago, while Passage 2 uses a secondary source to explain changes over many years. ✅C. Passage 1 discusses new businesses, while Passage 2 discusses old photographs.D. Passage 1 focuses on highways, while Passage 2 focuses on schools and parks.

Which TWO details from Passage 1 provide evidence that Cedar Creek was smaller in 1948 than it is today?
☑ There were only a few buildings, including a grocery store, post office, and gas station. ✅☑ Only a handful of cars could be seen in the photograph. ✅☐ A highway was built near the town in the 1980s.☐ The town added schools, parks, and public services.

According to Passage 2, the highway helped Cedar Creek grow because ______.
[ it reduced the number of businesses in town | it made travel easier and encouraged new businesses to move into the area ✅ | it replaced older neighborhoods | it created more primary sources for historians ]

writingPrompt.text: Which source would be more useful for learning about how Cedar Creek changed over time: the photograph or the historian's report? Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided. Standard: Primary and secondary sources can be used to show change over time.

passages.title: A Small Town Grows
passages.text:
1. Long ago, Pine Valley was a small community surrounded by farms. Most families grew crops or raised animals for a living. The town had a few stores, one school, and a small post office. People often traveled by bicycle, horse, or on foot.

2. As the years passed, more families moved to Pine Valley. New homes were built, and the population increased. To meet the needs of the growing community, workers constructed additional roads and opened new businesses. Soon, the town had restaurants, shops, and a larger grocery store.

3. Community leaders also worked to improve public services. A new library was built, and the school was expanded to serve more students. Parks and playgrounds were added so families would have places to gather and enjoy outdoor activities.

4. Today, Pine Valley is much larger than it was in the past. Many of the farms are still there, but the community now includes neighborhoods, businesses, and public buildings. The changes helped the town provide more opportunities and services for its residents.

passages.title: Changes Through Technology
passages.text:
5. While Pine Valley was growing, technology also changed the way people lived. Years ago, families communicated by writing letters or using landline telephones. Receiving information often took several days.

6. Today, many people use cell phones, computers, and the internet to communicate instantly. Students can research information online, and businesses can connect with customers in different places. Technology has helped community members stay informed and connected.

7. Transportation has changed as well. In the past, traveling to nearby towns could take a long time. As roads improved and more people owned cars, travel became faster and easier. Public transportation was also added to help residents reach schools, stores, and workplaces.

8. These technological changes have affected daily life in Pine Valley. People can communicate more quickly, travel more efficiently, and access information more easily than they could in the past. The community continues to change as new technologies are developed.

questions.text:
Which TWO ideas are discussed in both Passage 1 and Passage 2?
☑ Pine Valley changed over time to meet the needs of its residents. ✅☑ Improvements helped make life easier for people in the community. ✅☐ Most families still travel by horse or bicycle.☐ New farms were added throughout the community.

What is the main idea of Passage 2?
A. Students use computers to complete schoolwork.B. Technology and transportation changes have improved daily life in Pine Valley. ✅C. Landline telephones are better than cell phones.D. Public transportation is the most important invention.

In Passage 1, more roads and businesses were added because ______.
[ the population decreased over time | community leaders wanted to remove farms | more families moved to Pine Valley and the town needed to meet their needs ✅ | residents stopped using technology ]

writingPrompt.text: Which change had the greatest impact on Pine Valley: the growth of the community or the advances in technology?
